title: Recurrent Networks & Word Embeddings
blurb: ★★ Secondary · Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) process sequences by applying the **same** recurrence at every time step, carrying a hidden state forward in time. This chapter covers the recurrence formula and sequence types, the **vanishing/exploding gradient** problem and its LSTM/GRU remedies, and **word embeddings** — dense vectors that map language into a geometric space — including building RNN and embedding models in code.

knowledge_points.title: Recurrence, Sequence Types & BPTT
knowledge_points.recap: An RNN processes a sequence of vectors by applying a **recurrence formula at every time step**: $h_t = f_W(h_{t-1}, x_t)$, with the **same function and same weights W reused at every step**. The vanilla RNN uses a single hidden vector: $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t)$, optionally producing $y_t$. RNNs support flexible mappings: **one-to-many** (image captioning), **many-to-one** (sentiment classification), **many-to-many** (machine translation, video frame labeling), and **seq-to-seq** (many-to-one encoder + one-to-many decoder). Training uses **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** — forward through the whole sequence, then backward — and in practice **truncated BPTT** processes chunks, carrying hidden states forward but only backpropagating a limited number of steps.

questions.q: What is the key property of the parameters used across time steps in an RNN?
questions.answer: The **same function and the same weight matrix W are reused at every time step** — the recurrence $h_t = f_W(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ shares parameters over the whole sequence.

options.text: The same weights W are reused at every time step
Each time step learns its own independent weight matrix
Weights are shared only between the first and last step
The hidden state uses fixed, non-trainable weights

questions.q: Write the vanilla RNN recurrence and name the four sequence-processing patterns an RNN can implement, with one example each.
questions.answer:
**Recurrence:** the hidden state is updated as $$h_t = \tanh(W_{hh}\,h_{t-1} + W_{xh}\,x_t)$$ and an output can be read off as $y_t = W_{hy}\,h_t$.

**Sequence patterns:**
- **one-to-many**: single input -> sequence (e.g. **image captioning**).
- **many-to-one**: sequence -> single output (e.g. **sentiment classification**).
- **many-to-many**: sequence -> sequence (e.g. **machine translation**, or per-frame **video classification**).
- **seq-to-seq**: a many-to-one **encoder** compressing the input into a vector followed by a one-to-many **decoder** producing the output.

questions.q: What is **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, and why is **truncated BPTT** used in practice?
questions.answer: **BPTT** trains an RNN by unrolling it over the sequence: run a **full forward pass through the entire sequence to compute the loss**, then a **full backward pass** to compute gradients. For long sequences this is expensive in memory and compute and delays each update. **Truncated BPTT** instead runs forward and backward over **chunks** of the sequence: the hidden state is **carried forward in time indefinitely**, but gradients are only **backpropagated over a smaller fixed number of steps**, making training tractable while still capturing recent context.

questions.q: In a character-level language model over the vocabulary [h, e, l, o], how are characters fed in and how is text generated at test time?
questions.answer: Each character is fed in as a **one-hot vector**, and the RNN predicts a **softmax distribution over the next character** at each step. At test time you **sample one character from the softmax, then feed that sampled character back in as the next input**, repeating to generate text one character at a time. Training on 'hello' teaches the model to predict 'e' after 'h', 'l' after 'e', and so on.

knowledge_points.title: Vanishing/Exploding Gradients, LSTM & GRU Gates
knowledge_points.recap: Backpropagating through a vanilla RNN repeatedly multiplies by $W_{hh}^T$ (and the tanh derivative). Over many steps this makes the gradient of the early hidden states depend on **many factors of W**: if the **largest singular value of W is > 1** the gradient **explodes**; if it is **< 1** the gradient **vanishes**. Exploding gradients are handled by **gradient clipping** (scale the gradient when its norm is too large); vanishing gradients require **changing the architecture**. The **LSTM** adds a cell state and four gates: **f (forget)** — whether to erase the cell; **i (input)** — whether to write to the cell; **g (gate/candidate)** — how much to write; **o (output)** — how much of the cell to reveal. The additive cell-state path gives an uninterrupted gradient flow. The **GRU** is a lighter variant with fewer gates.

questions.q: When repeatedly backpropagating through a vanilla RNN, what determines whether gradients vanish or explode?
questions.answer: The **largest singular value of the recurrent weight matrix W**: if it is **> 1** the gradients **explode**, and if it is **< 1** the gradients **vanish**, because the gradient of the early states involves many repeated factors of W.

options.text: Largest singular value of W > 1 -> exploding; < 1 -> vanishing
The batch size chosen during training
Whether the inputs are one-hot encoded

questions.q: Name the four gates of an LSTM and state what each controls.
questions.answer:
- **f — Forget gate**: whether to **erase** the cell state.
- **i — Input gate**: whether to **write** to the cell.
- **g — Gate (candidate) gate**: **how much** to write to the cell.
- **o — Output gate**: how much of the cell to **reveal** as the hidden state.

Gates f, i, o use **sigmoid** (values in [0,1] act as soft switches) and the candidate g uses **tanh**. The additive update of the cell state gives a gradient highway that mitigates vanishing gradients — the reason LSTMs handle long-term dependencies better than vanilla RNNs.

questions.q: Which technique specifically addresses the **exploding** gradient problem in RNNs?
questions.answer: **Gradient clipping** — if the gradient's norm is too large, scale it down so its norm stays within a threshold. (Vanishing gradients are instead addressed by changing the architecture, e.g. to an LSTM/GRU.)

options.text: Gradient clipping (scale the gradient if its norm is too big)

questions.q: Why does an LSTM cope with long-term dependencies better than a vanilla RNN? Mention the GRU as an alternative.
questions.answer: The LSTM maintains a separate **cell state** updated **additively** and gated by the forget/input gates, so gradients can flow backward through many time steps **without being repeatedly multiplied by W and squashed by tanh** — avoiding the vanishing gradient that cripples the vanilla RNN. The **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** achieves a similar effect with **fewer gates** (merging cell and hidden state and combining gates), giving comparable performance with less computation.

knowledge_points.title: Word Embeddings & Building Sequence Models
knowledge_points.recap: A **word embedding** is a low-dimensional, **dense** floating-point vector associated with a word (typically 8-1024 dimensional), as opposed to sparse one-hot vectors. The goal is that **geometric relationships reflect semantic relationships**: synonyms map to nearby vectors and meaningful directions emerge (e.g. king - man + woman ~ queen). Embeddings can be **learned jointly** with the task (starting random, tuned by backprop) or loaded as **pre-trained** vectors (word2vec, GloVe). In Keras the **Embedding layer** is a dictionary lookup mapping integer word indices to vectors: it takes a 2D integer tensor `(samples, sequence_length)` and returns a 3D tensor `(samples, sequence_length, embedding_dim)`, which can then feed a `SimpleRNN`/`LSTM` or be flattened into Dense layers.

questions.q: What is a **word embedding**, how does it differ from a one-hot representation, and what famous property makes it useful? Name the two ways to obtain embeddings.
questions.answer:
A **word embedding** is a **low-dimensional dense vector** (e.g. 8-1024 dims) associated with a word, versus a high-dimensional **sparse one-hot** vector that carries no notion of similarity. Embeddings **map language into a geometric space where distance/direction reflect semantics**: synonyms land close together and meaningful directions appear — the classic example being **king - man + woman ~ queen** (also 'gender' and 'plural' vectors).

Two ways to obtain them:
1. **Learn jointly** with the target task — start from random vectors and tune them by **backpropagation** (an `Embedding` layer).
2. **Load pre-trained embeddings** computed on another task (e.g. **word2vec**, GloVe).

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to build the model (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Embedding, SimpleRNN, Dense

max_features = 10000  # vocabulary size

model = Sequential()
model.add(Embedding(max_features, 32))   # (samples, seq) -> (samples, seq, 32)
model.add(SimpleRNN(32))                 # returns last hidden state (samples, 32)
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))  # binary classification

model.compile(optimizer='rmsprop',
              loss='binary_crossentropy',
              metrics=['acc'])
```
By default `SimpleRNN` returns **only the last time step's output**; set `return_sequences=True` to output the full sequence (needed when stacking recurrent layers). The final **sigmoid + binary_crossentropy** matches the positive/negative review task.
questions.extend:
The PyTorch equivalent uses `nn.Embedding` + `nn.RNN`:
```python
import torch.nn as nn

class RNNClassifier(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab=10000, emb=32, hidden=32):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab, emb)
        self.rnn = nn.RNN(emb, hidden, batch_first=True)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)
    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.embed(x)          # (B, T, emb)
        _, h = self.rnn(x)         # h: (1, B, hidden)
        return self.fc(h.squeeze(0))  # logits; use BCEWithLogitsLoss
```

variants.code:
```python
model = Sequential()
model.add(____(1)(max_features, 32))     # word id -> 32-dim vector
model.add(____(2)(32))                    # recurrent layer (last hidden state)
model.add(Dense(1, activation=____(3)))
```

```python
model.add(Embedding(____(1), ____(2)))    # vocab size, embedding dim
model.add(SimpleRNN(32))
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))
model.compile(optimizer='rmsprop', loss=____(3), metrics=['acc'])
```

```python
model.add(Embedding(max_features, 32))
model.add(SimpleRNN(32, return_sequences=____(1)))  # feed next recurrent layer
model.add(SimpleRNN(32))
model.add(Dense(1, activation=____(2)))
```

blanks.hint: output full sequence to stack layers

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to build the model (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Embedding, Flatten, Dense

maxlen = 20
model = Sequential()
model.add(Embedding(10000, 8, input_length=maxlen))  # -> (samples, maxlen, 8)
model.add(Flatten())                                  # -> (samples, maxlen*8)
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))

model.compile(optimizer='rmsprop',
              loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['acc'])
```
`input_length=maxlen` fixes the sequence length so the 3D embedding output `(samples, maxlen, 8)` has a **known static shape** and can be **Flattened** into a fixed-size `(samples, maxlen*8)` vector for the Dense layer. Limitation: flattening then a single Dense treats each word **independently**, ignoring word order — adding recurrent or 1D-conv layers on top captures inter-word structure.

variants.code:
```python
model.add(____(1)(10000, 8, input_length=maxlen))  # -> (samples, maxlen, 8)
model.add(____(2)())                                # -> (samples, maxlen*8)
model.add(Dense(1, activation=____(3)))
```

```python
model.add(Embedding(____(1), ____(2), input_length=maxlen))
model.add(Flatten())
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))
model.compile(optimizer='rmsprop', loss=____(3), metrics=['acc'])
```

questions.q: An Embedding layer maps a vocabulary of 10000 words to 32-dimensional vectors, followed by a SimpleRNN with 32 hidden units fed 32-dim inputs. Compute the number of parameters in (a) the Embedding layer and (b) the SimpleRNN layer.
questions.answer:
**(a) Embedding:** it is a lookup table of size vocab x embedding_dim, so $10000 \times 32 = \mathbf{320000}$ parameters.

**(b) SimpleRNN:** with input dim $D=32$ and hidden dim $H=32$, the parameters are $W_{xh}$ ($D \times H$), $W_{hh}$ ($H \times H$) and bias ($H$): $32\cdot32 + 32\cdot32 + 32 = 1024 + 1024 + 32 = \mathbf{2080}$.

questions.q: The lab also builds a 1D convnet (Conv1D + MaxPooling1D) over embedded sequences instead of an RNN. Give one advantage of this approach.
questions.answer: A **1D convnet** over embeddings runs **much faster** than a recurrent network (convolutions parallelize across time steps, whereas an RNN is sequential) while reaching **competitive accuracy** on word-level sentiment classification. It can also afford **larger convolution windows** (size 7 or 9) since a 1D window of size k contains only k feature vectors, making it a cheap, fast alternative to an LSTM.

knowledge_points.title: ✍️ Code Fill-in-the-Blank
knowledge_points.recap: Type the missing tokens to build an Embedding + RNN sentiment model.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** to build the model (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Embedding, SimpleRNN, LSTM, Dense
model = Sequential()
model.add(Embedding(max_features, 32))
model.add(LSTM(32))          # or SimpleRNN(32)
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))
model.compile(optimizer='rmsprop', loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['acc'])
```
questions.extend: Memory: Embedding → LSTM/SimpleRNN → Dense(1, sigmoid) + binary_crossentropy.

variants.code:
```python
model.add(____(1)(max_features, 32))    # word id -> dense vector
model.add(____(2)(32))                   # recurrent layer that remembers context
model.add(Dense(1, activation=____(3)))
model.compile(optimizer='rmsprop', loss=____(4), metrics=['acc'])
```

```python
model.add(Embedding(max_features, 32))
model.add(____(1)(32, 7, activation='relu'))   # 1-D conv over the sequence
model.add(____(2)())                            # pool over time -> (samples, 32)
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))
```

knowledge_points.title: 🔮 SS26 Likely Exam Questions (predicted)
knowledge_points.recap: Predicted, exam-style questions in the professor's format — concept-first, plus a Keras code item where relevant. Not from a past paper; use to rehearse likely SS26 topics.

questions.q:
(a) Why do **vanilla RNNs** suffer from vanishing/exploding gradients over long sequences?
(b) How does an **LSTM** fix the *vanishing* case — name the gates and the mechanism.
(c) **Name the remedy you would use for the *exploding* case, and one you would NOT** (and why).
questions.answer:
**(a)** BPTT repeatedly multiplies the gradient by the recurrent matrix $W_{hh}^{\!\top}$ (and the $\tanh$ derivative). The early-state gradient therefore carries **many repeated factors of $W$**: if the **largest singular value of $W>1$** it **explodes**, if **$<1$** it **vanishes**.

**(b)** The **LSTM** adds a **cell state** updated **additively** and controlled by four gates — **f (forget)** erase the cell, **i (input)** whether to write, **g (candidate)** how much to write, **o (output)** how much to reveal. The additive cell path is a **gradient highway**: gradients flow back through many steps **without being repeatedly multiplied by $W$ and squashed**, so long-term dependencies survive. (The **GRU** does the same with fewer gates.)

**(c)** *Would use:* **gradient clipping** — rescale the gradient when its norm exceeds a threshold; it directly caps explosion. *Would NOT use:* clipping is **not** a fix for *vanishing* gradients — for those you must **change the architecture** (LSTM/GRU); scaling up a vanishing gradient by clipping does nothing.
questions.extend: Gates f, i, o use **sigmoid** (soft 0/1 switches); candidate g uses **tanh**. GRU merges cell and hidden state and combines gates.

questions.q:
(a) What is a **word embedding** and how does it differ from a **one-hot** vector?
(b) What benefit does an embedding offer that one-hot encoding cannot, with a classic example?
(c) **Name the two ways to obtain embeddings**, and say when you would prefer each.
questions.answer:
**(a)** A **word embedding** is a **low-dimensional dense** float vector (typically 8–1024 dims) per word; a **one-hot** vector is huge (vocabulary-sized), **sparse**, and every pair is equidistant, so it encodes **no similarity**.

**(b)** Embeddings map words into a **geometric space where distance and direction reflect meaning** — synonyms land close together and meaningful directions emerge, the classic example being $\text{king}-\text{man}+\text{woman}\approx\text{queen}$. One-hot vectors cannot express any such relationship.

**(c)** (1) **Learn jointly** with the task via a trainable **Embedding layer** (start random, tune by backprop) — preferred when you have **enough task data**. (2) **Load pre-trained** vectors such as **word2vec** or **GloVe** — preferred when your **dataset is small**, since the semantics were learned on a large external corpus.
questions.extend: In Keras the `Embedding` layer is a lookup table of shape (vocab × embedding_dim): it maps `(samples, seq_len)` integers to `(samples, seq_len, embedding_dim)`.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** for the Embedding + LSTM sentiment model (rotating variant each visit):
questions.answer:
```python
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Embedding, LSTM, Dense

max_features = 10000

model = Sequential()
model.add(Embedding(max_features, 32))     # (samples, seq) -> (samples, seq, 32)
model.add(LSTM(32))                        # long-range context, avoids vanishing grad
model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))  # binary sentiment

model.compile(optimizer='rmsprop',
              loss='binary_crossentropy',
              metrics=['acc'])

model.fit(x_train, y_train,
          epochs=10, batch_size=128,
          validation_data=(x_val, y_val))

loss, acc = model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
print('test acc:', acc)
```
Key points: **Embedding → LSTM → Dense(1, sigmoid)**, **binary_crossentropy** loss, **validation_data** in `fit`, **evaluate on the test set**. Chose **LSTM over SimpleRNN** because its gated cell state mitigates the **vanishing gradient**, capturing longer-range dependencies. To stack recurrent layers, set `return_sequences=True` on all but the last.
questions.extend: A cheaper alternative is a **Conv1D + MaxPooling1D** stack over the embeddings — it parallelizes across time (much faster than an RNN) with competitive accuracy.

```python
model.add(Dense(1, activation=____(3)))
```

```python
loss, acc = model.____(3)(x_test, y_test)
```

```python
model.add(LSTM(32, return_sequences=____(1)))  # feed next recurrent layer

model.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))
```
